text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 15 September 2020 

Dr Aisha Karim 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Aisha Karim, 

Morgan Ellis, DOB: 22-03-1992, Hospital No: H845920 NHS No. 1039485726  
Flat 12, 4 Brookview Court, Peckham, London SE15 3AB  

Diagnosis: 

Recurrent seizures with variable semiology; pattern and classification remain indeterminate at this stage. 

Aetiology: 

No structural lesion identified on prior imaging; additional investigations pending. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Migraine without aura; vitamin D insufficiency on supplementation. 

Management Plan: 

- Increase Levetiracetam from 1,000 mg twice daily to 1,500 mg twice daily.  
- Initiate Clobazam 5 mg nocte for two weeks, then increase to 10 mg nocte if tolerated.  
- Arrange ambulatory EEG (72-hour) with diary correlation and request repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol.  
- Bloods today: FBC, U&E, LFT, Levetiracetam level, vitamin D.  
- Safety: advised no driving and discussed SUDEP risk; provided written information.  
- Occupational adjustments: I will provide a letter to his employer supporting workload moderation and flexible scheduling due to clear workday clustering.  
- Follow-up in 8 weeks in clinic; sooner if events escalate. 

Since his last appointment, there has been a noticeable worsening in seizure control. He reports that episodes now occur in short bursts predominantly on workdays, often towards the late afternoon after prolonged desk-based tasks and meetings. He notes a cyclical pattern with a premenstrual week increase in events, described by him as a "pre-period upswing in episodes", which aligns with diary entries showing a rise in frequency during the days preceding menstruation. Over the past six weeks he has experienced three clusters requiring recovery time off work, each comprising two to four brief events over 24–48 hours. No clear nocturnal predominance. Post-event fatigue and headache are prominent. 

Precipitants identified by the patient include high workload, sustained screen exposure, and inadequate sleep. He denies alcohol excess. There have been no injuries or tongue bites. Witness accounts describe brief behavioural arrest with reduced responsiveness and occasional right-hand automatisms, followed by disorientation. Duration typically 30–90 seconds. No clear warning has been identified, though he sometimes reports a rising sense of internal "pressure" shortly before episodes. 

I have provided guidance on stress management, sleep hygiene, and structured breaks. He is maintaining a detailed seizure and trigger diary, which is being used to schedule the ambulatory EEG. We discussed medication options and the above adjustments were agreed. I also encouraged him to ensure timely meals and hydration during workdays, and I will support phased workload reduction with his employer to mitigate the work-related clustering he reports. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the past six weeks he has experienced three clusters requiring recovery time off work, each comprising two to four brief events over 24–48 hours.

attributes.Label: 1 cluster per 2 weeks, 2 to 4 per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: over the past six weeks there were three clusters, each with two to four brief events over 24–48 hours. Calculate cluster rate: 3 clusters / 6 weeks = 0.5 cluster per week = 1 cluster per 2 weeks. Per-cluster event count is 2 to 4. No other specific numeric frequencies are provided that supersede this.
seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per 2 weeks, 2 to 4 per cluster
Over the past six weeks he has experienced three clusters requiring recovery time off work, each comprising two to four brief events over 24–48 hours.
reference: Late luteal phase seizure exacerbations noted

flaws.description: Gold (1 cluster per 2 weeks, 2 to 4 per cluster) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
